risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: This explicitly describes a proper random allocation method (computer-generated random tables), which is sufficient to judge the sequence generation as truly random.

Quote: "Patients were randomly assigned to either groups – MBSR group or TAU group – using computer-generated random tables." Comment: The paper reports random assignment but does not describe any allocation concealment procedure (e.g., central allocation, opaque sealed envelopes, or identical containers). Computer-generated random tables relate to sequence generation, not concealment, so foreknowledge by recruiters cannot be ruled out.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Summary: The intervention was an individually delivered MBSR program versus treatment-as-usual, with no statement that participants or the personnel delivering the intervention were blinded. The paper describes active participation in the mindfulness sessions and home practice, making blinding of participants/personnel implausible. Comment: Because outcomes included self-reported anxiety, depression, and perceived stress, knowledge of allocation could plausibly influence results; therefore the study is at high risk of performance bias.

Quote: "The Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS) was used to assess anxiety and depressive symptoms... Perceived stress was assessed using the Perceived Stress Scale (PSS). Physical parameters included measurement of BP and BMI. BP of the patients was measured either by the cardiologist or a physician..." Comment: The outcome assessment included patient self-report instruments (HADS, PSS), so the patient was the assessor for key outcomes. Participants were not blinded in this MBSR vs TAU trial, and self-reported anxiety, depression, and stress are highly susceptible to bias from knowledge of allocation. Although BP/BMI are more objective, the domain is judged at the level of the most vulnerable outcome, and the presence of unblinded subjective outcomes makes detection bias high.

Quote: "Of the 30 patients, there were a total of 17 available at 3-month follow-up (12 in the MBSR and five in the TAU group)." Comment: Follow-up attrition was substantial overall (13/30 missing = 43% total loss) and highly imbalanced between arms (3/15 missing in MBSR = 20% vs 10/15 missing in TAU = 67%, a 47 percentage point difference), which crosses the hard thresholds for high risk. The paper states that baseline mean scores of those available versus unavailable at follow-up were compared and found not significantly different, but this does not offset the large and differential missing outcome data. Summary: The study also says follow-up was only "attempted for as many patients available," indicating incomplete outcome data were present at the relevant time point and were not handled with a robust missing-data method.
